feature_name: C64x+ VLIW core and instruction set
description: The device integrates a TMS320C64x+ fixed-point DSP core with an eight-issue VLIW pipeline. It supports software pipelining, saturation arithmetic, bit manipulation, and SIMD on 16-bit lanes. The core can dispatch up to eight 32-bit instructions per cycle across multiple functional units. L1 caches reduce latency while L2 can be configured as cache or SRAM to balance performance and determinism. The instruction set and execution model are defined in the CPU reference for predictable real-time behavior.
evidence: Device overview; Functional architecture summary; CPU: SPRU732
dependencies: None; optional cache and memory configuration during init
Remarks: Application code should follow TI calling conventions and consider pipeline hazards; warm instruction/data caches before latency-critical ISRs.

description: An on-chip L1 Program (L1P) cache stores recently executed instructions to reduce fetch latency. Typical size is 32 KB with line-based allocation and replacement controlled by the core. It is transparent to software but benefits from layout that improves locality and prefetch behavior. Cache-friendly code placement enhances loop performance and reduces stalls. The L1P cache works alongside the L2 memory which can augment instruction storage as SRAM or cache.
evidence: Device overview: Caches and on-chip memory; Functional architecture summary
dependencies: None; optional cache configuration via startup code
Remarks: Keep tight loops and hot paths contiguous to maximize hit rate; consider locking critical code in L2 SRAM if determinism is required.

description: An on-chip L1 Data (L1D) cache accelerates access to frequently used data. It typically provides 32 KB capacity and interfaces tightly with the load/store units. Software should structure data to encourage spatial and temporal locality for best results. DMA traffic and peripheral FIFOs should be managed to avoid cache-coherency hazards. L1D cooperates with L2 which can be partly or fully configured as SRAM for deterministic buffers.
evidence: Device overview: Caches and on-chip memory; Functional architecture summary
dependencies: None; consider cache maintenance when using DMA
Remarks: Align and pad data structures to cache line boundaries; use non-cacheable regions or writeback/invalidate around DMA buffers as needed.

description: The L2 memory can be configured as SRAM, cache, or a mix to balance capacity and determinism. Using L2 as SRAM allows locking of critical code and data outside of cache replacement effects. As cache, it extends the working set reachable at low latency compared to external memory. System attributes and mapping determine which external addresses are cacheable. Proper partitioning of L2 is foundational to predictable real-time behavior.
evidence: Device overview: Caches and on-chip memory; Functional architecture summary
dependencies: Startup configuration of L2; memory attribute setup as applicable
Remarks: Choose L2 partition early and hold constant across the product; reserve dedicated L2 SRAM for ISRs, stacks, and DMA descriptors when determinism is required.

description: The device integrates a DDR2/mDDR controller compliant with JEDEC, providing access to external SDRAM. Initialization requires programming timing registers and issuing the JEDEC power-up and mode register sequence. Refresh, drive strength, and VTP calibration (if applicable) must be configured for board-specific memory. After initialization, a memory pattern test is recommended to validate timing margins. The controller exposes registers for configuration, status, and maintenance operations.
evidence: DDR2 controller: SDRAM_CFG, SDRAM_TIMx, SDRAM_REFRESH; Typical initialization sequences
dependencies: PSC enable for DDR2 controller; stable PLL/clock; board-specific DDR2 parameters
Remarks: Program timing per the memory datasheet; ensure VTP calibration where required; verify initialization with a pattern test before enabling caches and DMA.

description: EDMA3 provides high-throughput, non-blocking data movement between memory and peripherals. Transfers are described by PaRAM sets supporting linked and chained scatter-gather operations. Channels map to queues and transfer controllers that arbitrate bandwidth. Interrupts signal completion and errors via IPR/ICR and CCERR registers. QDMA enables CPU-triggered copies with minimal overhead.
evidence: EDMA3 overview; Registers: DCHMAP, DMAQNUM, EER/EESR, IPR/ICR, CCERR
dependencies: PSC enable for EDMA CC/TC; INTC mapping; optional peripheral event routing
Remarks: Use linked PaRAM for scatter-gather; clear completion in ICR after reading IPR; place PaRAM/descriptors in coherent memory and align as recommended.

description: The Ethernet subsystem implements a Layer-2 switch with two external SGMII ports and one CPU port. It includes an Address Lookup Engine (ALE), VLAN and QoS support, and extensive statistics counters. An MDIO controller manages external PHY discovery and link configuration. Packet I/O uses a DMA-based interface with descriptor rings. Proper initialization brings up ports, configures VLAN/ALE entries, and enables RX/TX paths.
evidence: 3PSW Ethernet: ALE/VLAN/statistics; MDIOUSERACCESS/MDIOCONTROL; Packet DMA interface
dependencies: PSC enable for 3PSW and packet DMA; PINMUX; MDIO clock; external PHYs; EDMA/packet DMA setup
Remarks: Initialize MDIO and confirm PHY links before enabling traffic; configure ALE for host MACs and VLAN policy; ensure descriptor rings are cache coherent.

description: The PCI block supports host-master and slave transactions for connecting to a PCI host. Software configures the local configuration header, BARs, and command/status to expose internal windows. Interrupts and DMA cooperate to achieve high throughput for bulk transfers. Proper reset sequencing and host negotiation are required at bring-up. Address translation must match the host’s view for correct accesses.
evidence: PCI: configuration header, BARs, command/status; Typical initialization sequences
dependencies: PSC enable; host reset sequencing; BAR/window setup; EDMA for bulk movement
Remarks: Coordinate reset with the host and validate BAR mappings; use EDMA for large moves; confirm endianness and alignment expectations across the link.

description: The UHPI allows an external host to directly access the DSP memory map. Control registers manage bus width, resets, and ready/hold handshaking. It supports interrupt-driven or polled transactions depending on system design. Integration simplifies host boot and debug scenarios. Access arbitration must be considered to avoid contention with the core and DMA.
evidence: UHPI: HPIC, HPIA, HPID; Peripheral user’s guide references
dependencies: PSC enable; host timing and handshaking; interrupt routing if used
Remarks: Program HPIC for correct width and reset state before enabling; validate handshake timing; isolate shared regions to avoid race conditions.

description: VLYNQ provides a point-to-point serial link that maps remote resources into the local address space. It enables chip-to-chip connectivity with memory-mapped semantics. Configuration defines lane rates, framing, and the address windows. Interrupts and error status support robust operation. Both ends must be configured consistently for link bring-up.
evidence: Peripherals overview mentions VLYNQ; Functional architecture summary
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; remote endpoint configuration; address window mapping
Remarks: Negotiate link parameters with the peer and verify window mappings; test with small transactions before enabling DMA over the link.

description: McASP supports I2S, TDM, and DIT modes with independent clock and frame sync generators. Serializers can be flexibly assigned to transmit or receive. Data flows through XBUF/RBUF FIFOs with status and interrupts. Clocks and pins are controlled via GBLCTL, PINCTL, and SRCTL registers. EDMA is typically used to service RX/TX with minimal CPU overhead.
evidence: McASP: GBLCTL, PINCTL, SRCTLn, XBUFn/RBUFn, XINT/RINT
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; EDMA channel setup; INTC mapping
Remarks: Sequence McASP state machine carefully when starting and stopping streams; verify clock polarity and frame timing; size EDMA bursts to frame boundaries.

description: The SPI module provides master/slave transfers with programmable clock polarity and phase. Formats define word length and bit order for flexible peripheral interfacing. Data moves through SPIDATx and SPIBUF with optional interrupts. Pin control registers manage chip-selects and I/O direction. EDMA can be attached for high-rate streaming.
evidence: SPI: SPIGCR0/1, SPIFMTx, SPIDATx, SPIBUF, SPIDELAY, SPIINT
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; chip-select wiring; optional EDMA/interrupts
Remarks: Reset and configure SPIGCR0/1 before enabling; choose CPOL/CPHA to match the slave; consider EDMA for long transfers and align buffers.

description: The I2C module supports standard and fast modes in master or slave operation. Clocking is set via prescaler and SCL low/high timing registers. Transfers are controlled by ICMDR with status in ICSTR. Interrupts cover ready, NACK, arbitration lost, bus busy, and completion. External pull-ups on SDA/SCL are required for reliable signaling.
evidence: I2C: ICOAR, ICSAR, ICPSC, ICCLKL/ICCLKH, ICCNT, ICMDR, ICSTR, ICIER
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; external pull-ups; INTC mapping if interrupts used
Remarks: Handle NACK and arbitration lost per ICSTR sequencing; program ICCNT for multi-byte frames; deassert ICMDR.STP only after the last byte is acknowledged.

description: The UART is 16550-like with configurable word length, parity, and stop bits. FIFOs buffer RX/TX to reduce interrupt rate. Baud is generated from the input clock using DLL/DLH divisors. Module enable and reset are controlled by PWREMU_MGNT. Interrupts or EDMA can service data depending on throughput requirements.
evidence: UART: THR/RBR, LCR, FCR, DLL/DLH, IER/IIR, PWREMU_MGNT
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; clock reference for baud; INTC mapping
Remarks: Reset FIFOs before enabling; validate baud error versus the clock; use EDMA only when sustained high data rates justify the setup cost.

description: The GPIO module exposes up to 32 pins configurable as inputs or outputs. Direction is set per bit and data can be written via SET_DATA/CLR_DATA or read via IN_DATA. Edge detection supports rising and falling edges with latched status. Banked and optional direct interrupts route events to the INTC. Registers also include bank enable and status for interrupt control.
evidence: GPIO: DIR, SET_DATA/CLR_DATA, IN_DATA, SET_RIS_TRIG/CLR_RIS_TRIG, BINTEN, INTSTAT01 (SPRUF95)
dependencies: PSC enable; PINMUX; INTC mapping; optional EDMA trigger usage
Remarks: Enable the module via PSC before access to avoid bus hangs; clear edge flags after servicing; debounce externally if needed.

description: The general-purpose timer provides a 64-bit counter that can be split into two 32-bit timers. Period and control registers configure one-shot or periodic operation. Interrupts can be generated on compare or overflow events. The timer can be used for timestamps, periodic ticks, PWM, and capture. Proper prescaler and mode selection are required for target timing.
evidence: Timer64: TIM12/TIM34, PRD12/PRD34, TCR, TGCR, INTCTL
dependencies: PSC enable; INTC mapping; optional pinmux for capture/PWM
Remarks: Select 64-bit or dual 32-bit mode early; compute prescalers from the known input clock; keep ISR short and defer work to tasks.

description: The main PLL multiplies the reference oscillator to generate CPU and system clocks. Dividers create peripheral clocks that feed modules such as GPIO and timers. Reprogramming requires a defined sequence with command and status handshaking. Clock stability must be ensured before enabling peripherals or external memory. Downstream timing assumptions depend on the chosen PLL rates.
evidence: PLL: PLLCTL, PLLM, PLLDIVx, PLLCMD, PLLSTAT; Typical initialization sequences
dependencies: Stable reference clock; correct programming order; interact with PSC sequencing
Remarks: Follow the documented lock/unlock sequence and poll PLLSTAT; update peripheral dividers coherently; validate derived clocks with a scope during bring-up.

description: The PSC manages power and clock gating of modules grouped into domains. Each peripheral has an LPSC state machine that must be transitioned to ENABLE before register access. Transitions are requested via PTCMD and observed through PTSTAT and MDSTAT. Proper sequencing avoids bus stalls and reduces power. Software uses the PSC throughout initialization and when changing active peripherals.
evidence: PSC: PTCMD, PTSTAT, MDCTL[n], MDSTAT[n]
dependencies: Required by all peripheral drivers; domain sequencing per SoC integration
Remarks: Always enable a module via PSC before touching its registers; poll for transition complete; disable unused modules to save power.

description: Many pins are multiplexed between peripheral functions and GPIO. Access to pinmux registers typically requires unlocking KICK registers with device-specific keys. Correct mux selection ensures signal availability and avoids conflicts. Boot configuration may set initial mux states, but software should reconfigure after bring-up. Reserved fields must be preserved when writing pinmux registers.
evidence: Device configuration/pinmux: KICK0/KICK1 (unlock), PINMUXn; Boot configuration
dependencies: KICK unlock sequence; ordered with PSC enables to avoid unsafe pin toggling
Remarks: Plan a single, early pinmux configuration step; document and review conflicts; keep a board-specific mux table under revision control.

description: The device boots from sources such as I2C, SPI, UART, PCI, or HPI determined by strap pins. The ROM performs minimal clock and pin initialization to load a first-stage image. After transfer, control passes to application code in internal memory. Software typically reprograms PLL and enables required PSC modules. Exact capabilities can vary by ROM revision noted in the device manual.
evidence: Boot sources in Reset and boot; Boot pins; Post-boot notes
dependencies: Board strap pins; ROM revision; external boot media availability
Remarks: Keep the first-stage loader minimal and robust; hand off to a unified board init that programs PLL/PSC/DDR deterministically.

description: The C64x+ INTC maps up to 128 system events into 12 maskable CPU interrupts. Peripheral and EDMA events can be routed either directly or via an event combiner. Software defines priority by mapping and ISR design. Clearing peripheral status before acknowledging the INTC prevents retrigger. Efficient ISR prolog/epilog and cache awareness reduce latency.
evidence: Interrupt model: INTC overview; Peripheral events; EDMA completion/error
dependencies: Peripheral interrupt enables; INTC mapping; optional event combiner
Remarks: Centralize vector table setup; keep ISR code small and defer to tasks; audit that each ISR clears the correct peripheral flag first.

description: The core IDLE instruction gates the CPU clock until an enabled interrupt occurs. The PSC can further gate module or domain clocks to reduce consumption. Wake sources must remain active and routed through the INTC. Software coordinates entry and exit to maintain data integrity. Proper use extends battery life or reduces thermal load in static systems.
evidence: Low-power modes: IDLE; PSC domain gating in Power management
dependencies: Interrupt routing for wake; PSC domain configuration
Remarks: Use IDLE in wait loops instead of polling; validate that wake interrupts are not masked; ensure DMA activity is quiescent before gating domains.

description: A JTAG interface provides real-time debug, trace, and halt capabilities. Some peripherals include emulation-aware controls to keep clocks active during halt. Debug sessions must consider side effects on timers, DMA, and external links. Boot timing and emulator attach order can influence behavior. Following vendor guidance avoids interference with real-time operation.
evidence: Emulation and debug considerations; PWR/EMU-related notes
dependencies: External emulator; toolchain integration
Remarks: Avoid halting during active peripheral transfers; enable emulation-aware options where available; script common debug attach sequences.

description: Peripherals expose status and error flags that are often cleared by writing one to the bit. The EDMA provides completion and error reporting via IPR/ICR and CCERR. Robust drivers check, log, and clear these conditions promptly. Centralizing error paths simplifies diagnostics across modules. Documentation of expected versus fatal errors improves field triage.
evidence: EDMA IPR/ICR/CCERR; Peripheral status registers and clear sequences
dependencies: Per-driver ISR and polling paths; logging infrastructure
Remarks: Unify error-reporting conventions; always follow write-1-to-clear semantics; include counters and timestamps for recurring faults.

description: UNKNOWN
evidence: UNKNOWN
dependencies: UNKNOWN
Remarks: UNKNOWN

description: Memory attribute and address extension configuration controls cacheability and access properties for external regions. Proper setup ensures that DMA buffers and MMIO windows have the intended caching behavior. These settings complement L2 partitioning for balanced performance. Misconfiguration can lead to stale data or bus faults. Adjustments are typically performed during early boot.
evidence: Functional architecture summary notes MAR/MPAX for external addresses
dependencies: Privileged CPU configuration; coordinated with cache and DMA usage
Remarks: Establish attribute tables once during early init; mark MMIO non-cacheable; verify with targeted cacheability tests.}
